era_true.statement: Alternating current allows voltage to be stepped up or down using transformers.
Transformers operate on the principle of electromagnetic induction between two coils.
Higher transmission voltages reduce energy losses over long distances.
Nikola Tesla held the original patents for the polyphase alternating current system.
The rotating magnetic field is the operating principle behind the induction motor.
A resonant circuit reaches maximum amplitude at its natural frequency.
Radio waves travel at the speed of light through free space.
Electromagnetic waves were predicted by James Clerk Maxwell's equations.
Heinrich Hertz demonstrated the existence of electromagnetic waves in 1887.
A conductor carrying current generates a magnetic field around it.
Lightning is a natural discharge of static electricity in the atmosphere.
The Earth itself carries an electrical charge and can conduct current.
Wardenclyffe Tower was built on Long Island, New York.
Niagara Falls was the site of the first large-scale AC power station.
The Niagara Falls AC power station began operation in 1895.
A Tesla coil produces high-voltage, low-current, high-frequency alternating current.
Resonance can cause mechanical structures to vibrate with destructive amplitude.
Copper is a better electrical conductor than iron.
Electrical resistance in a wire generates heat proportional to the square of the current.
Direct current flows in one direction only.
Alternating current reverses direction periodically.
The frequency of AC power in the United States is sixty cycles per second.
Capacitors store energy in an electric field between two conductive plates.
Inductors store energy in a magnetic field generated by current flowing through a coil.
A vacuum tube can amplify electrical signals.
Fluorescent lamps operate by exciting gas molecules with electrical current.
The ionosphere reflects certain radio frequencies back toward the Earth's surface.
Wireless telegraphy preceded voice radio transmission.
Guglielmo Marconi transmitted a radio signal across the Atlantic Ocean in 1901.
The piezoelectric effect converts mechanical stress into electrical charge in certain crystals.
An antenna converts electromagnetic waves into electrical current and vice versa.
Electrical power equals voltage multiplied by current.
Ohm's law states that current equals voltage divided by resistance.
Faraday's law states that a changing magnetic field induces an electromotive force.
Lenz's law states that an induced current opposes the change that produced it.
The skin effect causes high-frequency current to flow near the surface of a conductor.
Three-phase alternating current delivers power more efficiently than single-phase current.
A dynamo converts mechanical energy into electrical energy.
An electric motor converts electrical energy into mechanical energy.
The dielectric strength of a material determines the voltage it can withstand before breakdown.
Grounding an electrical system provides a safe path for fault current.
X-rays were discovered by Wilhelm Röntgen in 1895.
Cathode rays are streams of electrons emitted in a vacuum tube.
The electron was identified as a subatomic particle by J.J. Thomson in 1897.
Superconductivity was discovered by Heike Kamerlingh Onnes in 1911.
The photoelectric effect demonstrates that light can eject electrons from a metal surface.
Radar uses reflected radio waves to detect the position of distant objects.
Electromagnetic induction was discovered by Michael Faraday in 1831.
The speed of light in a vacuum is roughly three hundred thousand kilometers per second.
An oscillating electric charge radiates electromagnetic waves.
The wavelength of an electromagnetic wave is inversely proportional to its frequency.
Impedance in an AC circuit combines resistance, capacitance, and inductance effects.
A step-up transformer increases voltage while decreasing current.
A step-down transformer decreases voltage while increasing current.
Eddy currents are loops of electrical current induced within conductors by a changing magnetic field.
Neon gas emits a red-orange glow when electrically excited.
The Earth completes one rotation on its axis every twenty-four hours.
The Earth orbits the Sun once every three hundred sixty-five and one-quarter days.
The Moon orbits the Earth and causes ocean tides through gravitational force.
Water freezes at zero degrees Celsius at standard atmospheric pressure.
Water boils at one hundred degrees Celsius at standard atmospheric pressure.
The speed of sound in air at sea level is roughly three hundred forty meters per second.
Light travels faster than sound.
The Pacific Ocean is the largest ocean on Earth.
Mount Everest is the tallest mountain above sea level on Earth.
The Sahara Desert is located in northern Africa.
The Amazon River flows through South America.
The Nile River flows through northeastern Africa.
The Panama Canal connects the Atlantic Ocean to the Pacific Ocean.
The Suez Canal connects the Mediterranean Sea to the Red Sea.
The Great Wall of China was built over many centuries to defend against northern invasions.
The Roman Empire once controlled territory across Europe, North Africa, and western Asia.
The printing press was developed by Johannes Gutenberg in the fifteenth century.
The French Revolution began in 1789.
The United States Declaration of Independence was signed in 1776.
Napoleon Bonaparte was defeated at the Battle of Waterloo in 1815.
The American Civil War lasted from 1861 to 1865.
The Eiffel Tower was completed in Paris in 1889.
World War One began in 1914 and ended in 1918.
The Treaty of Versailles was signed in 1919.
The Titanic sank in the North Atlantic Ocean in April 1912.
The Russian Revolution of 1917 overthrew the Tsarist government.
The Great Depression began with the stock market crash of 1929.
Adolf Hitler became Chancellor of Germany in 1933.
Germany invaded Poland on September 1, 1939, starting World War Two in Europe.
Japan attacked Pearl Harbor on December 7, 1941.
The United States entered World War Two after the attack on Pearl Harbor.
The Battle of Stalingrad was fought between German and Soviet forces.
London was heavily bombed by Germany during the Blitz of 1940 and 1941.
The League of Nations was established after World War One.
Serbia is located in the Balkan Peninsula of southeastern Europe.
Croatia borders the Adriatic Sea.
Belgrade is the capital of Serbia.
Tesla was born in Smiljan, in the Austrian Empire, in 1856.
The Austro-Hungarian Empire dissolved after World War One.
Albert Einstein published the special theory of relativity in 1905.
Radioactivity was discovered by Henri Becquerel in 1896.
Marie Curie received two Nobel Prizes, one in physics and one in chemistry.
Ernest Rutherford demonstrated that atoms have a dense central nucleus.
The periodic table organizes chemical elements by atomic number.
Gravity causes objects to accelerate toward the Earth at the same rate regardless of mass in a vacuum.
Sound cannot travel through a vacuum.
The human heart pumps blood through the circulatory system.
Oxygen is required for human respiration.
Nitrogen makes up the largest portion of Earth's atmosphere by volume.
The compass needle aligns with the Earth's magnetic field.
The North Magnetic Pole and the geographic North Pole are not at the same location.
Diamond is the hardest naturally occurring mineral.
Gold is a chemical element that resists corrosion and oxidation.
Mercury is a metal that is liquid at room temperature.
Helium is lighter than air and does not burn.
The Hindenburg airship was destroyed by fire in New Jersey in 1937.
Charles Lindbergh flew solo across the Atlantic Ocean in 1927.
The Wright brothers achieved powered heavier-than-air flight in 1903.
Thomas Edison established the first commercial power station in New York City in 1882.
Edison's Pearl Street Station delivered direct current electricity.
The Chicago World's Fair of 1893 was illuminated using alternating current.
Mark Twain and Nikola Tesla were personal acquaintances in New York City.
The human body conducts electricity.
Steel is an alloy composed primarily of iron and carbon.

era_false.statement: Direct current is more efficient than alternating current for long-distance power transmission.
Thomas Edison invented the alternating current induction motor.
Radio waves travel faster than light waves in a vacuum.
Guglielmo Marconi independently invented the radio without building on any prior work.
Electricity cannot pass through a vacuum under any circumstances.
The Earth's magnetic field points from geographic north to geographic south internally.
Copper is a poor conductor of electricity compared to rubber.
Transformers can step up or step down direct current voltage with equal ease.
Nikola Tesla was born in Budapest, Hungary.
The speed of light in a vacuum is approximately 100,000 miles per second.
Electromagnetic waves require a physical medium to propagate through space.
Benjamin Franklin proved that lightning is unrelated to electricity.
A rotating magnetic field cannot be produced using polyphase alternating currents.
Water is an excellent electrical insulator in all conditions.
The frequency of alternating current has no effect on transformer operation.
Heinrich Hertz demonstrated that electromagnetic waves travel slower than sound.
Niagara Falls was first harnessed for hydroelectric power using direct current generators.
Capacitors store energy in the form of magnetic fields.
The Tesla coil operates exclusively on direct current principles.
Michael Faraday discovered that magnetic fields have no relationship to electric currents.
Lightning strikes carry a maximum voltage of approximately 500 volts.
Aluminum is a better electrical conductor than silver.
James Clerk Maxwell's equations predicted that electromagnetic waves do not exist.
The 1893 World's Columbian Exposition in Chicago was illuminated entirely by gas lamps.
Resonance has no practical application in electrical circuits.
Inductors oppose changes in voltage rather than changes in current.
The polyphase alternating current system was invented by Thomas Edison in 1882.
Electrons flow from the positive terminal to the negative terminal in a circuit.
X-rays were discovered by Nikola Tesla before Wilhelm Röntgen announced his findings.
The Earth carries no electrical charge whatsoever.
Ohm's law states that current is inversely proportional to voltage.
Wardenclyffe Tower was located in Colorado Springs, Colorado.
Electromagnetic induction was discovered by Alessandro Volta in 1831.
A dynamo converts mechanical energy into chemical energy.
The vacuum tube was never used in radio technology before 1943.
Sound waves are a form of electromagnetic radiation.
Alternating current reverses direction once per full cycle.
The Atlantic Ocean was first crossed by radio signal in 1920.
Tungsten has a lower melting point than copper.
Fluorescent lighting operates exclusively on direct current.
The piezoelectric effect was discovered by Nikola Tesla in 1880.
Gravity is an electromagnetic phenomenon caused by charged particles.
The Westinghouse Electric Company was founded by Thomas Edison.
An electric motor converts electrical energy into sound energy exclusively.
Impedance in an AC circuit is determined solely by resistance.
The photoelectric effect was explained by Isaac Newton.
Radar technology relies on sound waves rather than radio waves.
Magnetic fields cannot exert force on moving charged particles.
The War of Currents was a dispute between Tesla and Alexander Graham Bell.
Nikola Tesla became a United States citizen in 1901.
Superconductivity was never observed before 1943.
The electron was discovered by James Watt in 1897.
Voltage is measured in units called amperes.
The aurora borealis is caused by chemical reactions in the upper atmosphere unrelated to magnetism.
Hydroelectric power plants convert solar energy directly into electricity.
The wavelength of radio waves is shorter than the wavelength of visible light.
An electromagnet loses all magnetic properties when current flows through it.
The first practical telephone was invented by Guglielmo Marconi.
Alternating current cannot be used to power electric motors.
The dielectric constant of a material has no effect on capacitance.
Nikola Tesla worked exclusively for Thomas Edison throughout his entire career.
Lightning never strikes the same location twice under any circumstances.
The ionosphere has no effect on radio wave propagation.
Electrical resistance in a wire decreases as the wire becomes thinner.
The first power plant at Niagara Falls began operation in 1920.
A solenoid carrying current does not produce a magnetic field.
Gamma rays have lower frequency than radio waves.
The three-phase electrical system requires six separate conductors minimum.
Wireless telegraphy was impossible before the invention of the transistor.
The unit of electrical resistance is named after Benjamin Franklin.
Cathode rays are streams of positively charged particles.
The Colorado Springs laboratory experiments took place in New York City.
An ammeter must be connected in parallel to measure current correctly.
The speed of electricity through a conductor equals the speed of individual electrons.
Faraday's law states that a changing electric field cannot induce a magnetic field.
The first commercial power station was built in London by Nikola Tesla.
Mercury is a solid at room temperature and cannot conduct electricity.
Lenz's law states that induced current flows to strengthen the change causing it.
The Van de Graaff generator was invented in the eighteenth century.
Alternating current frequency in the United States was standardized at 25 hertz.
Electrical power is calculated by dividing voltage by current.
The Tesla unit measures electrical current in the SI system.
Nitrogen gas is an excellent conductor of electricity under normal conditions.
The filament in an incandescent bulb is typically made of aluminum.
Radio waves cannot be reflected or refracted under any conditions.
The first transatlantic telegraph cable was laid in 1895.
A parallel circuit has the same current flowing through every branch.
The Leyden jar was invented in the twentieth century.
Coulomb's law states that electrical force is independent of distance between charges.
The Earth completes one rotation on its axis every 48 hours.
Magnetic monopoles were commonly observed in laboratory experiments before 1943.
An electric arc cannot occur in open air under normal atmospheric pressure.
The Eiffel Tower was built primarily as a radio transmission facility.
Nikola Tesla received the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1915.
Kirchhoff's current law states that currents entering a junction need not equal currents leaving.
Glass is a better electrical conductor than copper wire.
The principle of electromagnetic induction has no application in electric generators.
Samuel Morse invented the electric battery.
The wavelength of visible light is longer than the wavelength of radio waves.
Alternating current was first demonstrated publicly in the 1920s.
A voltmeter must be connected in series with a circuit to measure voltage.
The skin effect causes alternating current to concentrate at the center of a conductor.
Eddy currents are beneficial and never cause energy losses in electrical machines.
The Chicago World's Fair of 1893 occurred in the year 1903.
Absolute zero temperature is defined as zero degrees Celsius.
Nikola Tesla held fewer than ten patents throughout his entire lifetime.
The Atlantic Ocean is narrower than the Pacific Ocean at every latitude.
Steel is a non-magnetic material that cannot be magnetized under any conditions.
Electrical energy cannot be converted into mechanical energy.
The first hydroelectric power plant in the world was built in Germany in 1925.
Sound travels faster in a vacuum than in air.
A galvanometer is used to measure temperature in electrical systems.
The phenomenon of electrical resonance was disproven by experiments before 1900.
Direct current changes polarity sixty times per second.
The planet Mars has a larger diameter than Earth.
Electromagnetic waves were first predicted by André-Marie Ampère in 1895.
Rubber is widely used as a conductor in electrical wiring.
The Great Pyramid of Giza was constructed in the fifteenth century.
The human body is completely immune to electric shock at any voltage.
Thermal energy has no relationship to the motion of atoms or molecules.

era_believed.statement: Wireless power transmission through the Earth can be made commercially efficient at global scales.
The luminiferous aether exists as the medium through which electromagnetic waves propagate.
Wardenclyffe Tower was designed to transmit usable electrical power to any point on Earth.
The Earth's resonant frequency can be harnessed to transmit unlimited energy wirelessly.
Cosmic rays are produced by interstellar particles accelerated by extremely high voltages in space.
Alternating current at extremely high frequencies is completely harmless to the human body.
Electrons flow through conductors like fluid flows through a pipe with no quantum effects.
Radio signals can be transmitted to other planets using sufficiently powerful terrestrial transmitters.
The ionosphere acts as a perfect conductor for global wireless energy distribution.
Stationary waves in the Earth can carry power with negligible loss over any distance.
Ball lightning is a stable electromagnetic phenomenon that can be reproduced reliably in laboratories.
A sufficiently powerful oscillator is capable of splitting the Earth in two through mechanical resonance.
Electromagnetic radiation consists entirely of longitudinal waves through a physical medium.
Single-node vacuum tubes are the ultimate amplification technology for radio transmission.
Particle beams can be projected through open atmosphere over hundreds of miles as weapons.
Rotating magnetic fields were a completely novel discovery with no prior theoretical prediction.
Atoms are indivisible beyond the electron and nucleus and contain no further substructure.
Radiant energy from space can be captured in sufficient quantities to power civilization.
The human brain operates on electrical impulses that function identically to simple circuit signals.
Turbine designs can achieve thermodynamic efficiencies approaching one hundred percent.
Wireless transmission of electrical energy is more practical than a global wire-based grid.
Teleautomatons represent the highest possible form of autonomous machine intelligence.
Direct current power distribution is fundamentally inferior to alternating current in every application.
Superluminal energy transmission is possible through longitudinal electromagnetic waves.
Standing electromagnetic waves in the Earth-ionosphere cavity can transmit power without radiation loss.
The photoelectric effect can be fully explained by wave theory without invoking photons.
Vacuum tube technology will remain the basis of all electronic systems indefinitely.
Mechanical resonance can amplify energy input without any theoretical upper bound.
A death ray projecting concentrated particle beams through air is physically achievable.
Electromagnetic phenomena require no quantum mechanical description and are purely classical.
Radio communication works by exciting standing waves in the Earth rather than radiating into space.
Extremely low frequency signals propagate through the Earth with virtually zero attenuation.
Rotating magnetic fields can produce unlimited torque with perfect efficiency in motors.
Radar is an inferior technology compared to Tesla's proposed standing-wave detection systems.
The speed of light is determined by the properties of the luminiferous aether.
Electrical resonance in circuits has no fundamental physical limits on voltage amplification.
Transmission of images and video wirelessly will always require analog signal methods.
Cosmic rays originate from electrical discharges between celestial bodies at enormous voltages.
Bladeless turbines are inherently more efficient than bladed turbines for all applications.
Fluorescent lighting driven by high-frequency current is completely safe at any power level.
The Earth behaves as a perfectly smooth conductor for electrical transmission purposes.
Wireless power can be metered and directed to individual receivers with simple resonant tuning.
Electrical oscillations at the natural frequency of the Earth encounter zero resistance.
Scalar waves exist as a distinct form of electromagnetic propagation beyond Hertzian waves.
High-frequency electrical currents have inherent therapeutic and healing properties for human tissue.
The output of a magnifying transmitter can exceed its input through resonant amplification.
Polyphase alternating current systems represent the final and optimal form of power distribution.
Atmospheric electricity can be tapped as a free and inexhaustible energy source.
Electromagnetic energy can be transmitted through a single wire with no return path.
Vacuum is a perfect insulator that cannot conduct electricity under any circumstances.
Mechanical vibrations at the right frequency can destroy any structure regardless of its mass.
Radio waves and light are fundamentally different phenomena rather than the same spectrum.
The electron is the smallest possible unit of electric charge.
Pluto is the ninth and outermost planet of the solar system.
The universe is in a steady state and has existed eternally without expansion.
The Milky Way galaxy constitutes the entire universe.
Ulcers are caused primarily by stress and spicy food rather than bacterial infection.
Eugenics is a scientifically valid method for improving the human species.
The four fundamental tastes are sweet, sour, salty, and bitter.
Atoms are the smallest indivisible units of chemical elements.
The continent of Antarctica has no significant mineral or fossil resources.
The speed of light in vacuum is an absolute constant unaffected by gravitational fields.
Protein is the molecule responsible for hereditary transmission in living organisms.
Genes are made of protein rather than nucleic acid.
The neutron is a fundamental particle with no internal structure.
Chemical bonds form through the transfer or sharing of electrons with no quantum tunneling involved.
Mass and energy are separately conserved quantities that do not interchange in chemical reactions.
The proton is an indivisible elementary particle.
The universe contains roughly one hundred million stars total.
Mercury is the hottest planet in the solar system due to its proximity to the Sun.
The Sun generates energy through gravitational contraction rather than nuclear fusion alone.
Vitamin C supplementation can prevent the common cold.
The human body has exactly forty-eight chromosomes.
Lobotomy is an effective and scientifically sound treatment for mental illness.
Schizophrenia is caused primarily by poor parenting and childhood environment.
Asbestos is a safe and ideal material for building insulation and fireproofing.
DDT is harmless to wildlife and ecosystems when used as an agricultural pesticide.
Smoking tobacco has no established connection to lung cancer.
Radiation exposure at low doses is completely harmless to human health.
Continental drift is a fringe hypothesis unsupported by any plausible mechanism.
The ocean floor is ancient, static, and does not move or regenerate.
There are no tectonic plates beneath the Earth's surface driving geological change.
Mountains form exclusively through the cooling and contraction of the Earth's crust.
The deep ocean floor is flat, featureless, and biologically barren.
Handedness is a behavioral choice that can be corrected through training.
Left-handedness is a mild developmental defect that should be corrected in children.
Intelligence is determined almost entirely by heredity with negligible environmental influence.
Race is a precise biological category with clear genetic boundaries between groups.
The sound barrier is a physical wall that no aircraft can ever break through.
Rockets cannot function in the vacuum of space because they need air to push against.
Chemical rockets are the only feasible method of propulsion for reaching outer space.
No manmade object will ever achieve orbit around the Earth.
Manned flight beyond the Earth's atmosphere is physically impossible.
The far side of the Moon has features essentially identical to the near side.
Mars has a network of artificial canals built by an intelligent civilization.
Venus beneath its clouds likely has a warm, wet, habitable surface similar to Earth.
The Sun orbits the center of the Milky Way in a perfectly circular path.
All galaxies in the universe are moving randomly with no large-scale pattern of recession.
The age of the Earth is roughly two billion years.
The universe is roughly two billion years old.
Absolute zero temperature can be reached with sufficiently advanced refrigeration technology.
Superconductivity is a phenomenon limited to a few exotic metals near absolute zero.
Nuclear fission chain reactions cannot be controlled for sustained civilian power generation.
Heavier-than-air flight at speeds exceeding five hundred miles per hour is impractical.
Jet engines will never replace propeller-driven engines for commercial aviation.
Television will remain a novelty and never become a mass medium for entertainment.
Computers will always require enormous rooms and can never be made portable.
No more than a handful of computers will ever be needed in the entire world.
The Amazon rainforest is so vast that human activity could never significantly diminish it.
The global human population will stabilize well below three billion people.
Polio will remain an incurable and unpreventable disease for the foreseeable future.
Antibiotics will permanently eliminate bacterial infectious disease as a major cause of death.
Malaria is caused by breathing foul air from swamps rather than by parasitic infection alone.
Pellagra is an infectious disease rather than a nutritional deficiency.
Dental cavities are caused exclusively by mouth bacteria with no role for dietary sugar.
The appendix is a completely vestigial organ with no biological function whatsoever.
The human brain uses only ten percent of its total capacity.
Nerve cells in the adult human brain cannot regenerate or be replaced.
Memory is stored in a single localized region of the brain.
Infants do not feel pain because their nervous systems are insufficiently developed.

modern_true.statement: The transistor was invented at Bell Labs in 1947 by John Bardeen, Walter Brattain, and William Shockley.
The integrated circuit was independently developed by Jack Kilby and Robert Noyce in 1958 and 1959.
Superconducting materials carry electric current with zero resistance below a critical temperature.
The first programmable electronic digital computer, ENIAC, became operational in 1945.
High-voltage direct current transmission became commercially viable for long-distance power delivery after 1954.
The maser, a microwave amplification device using stimulated emission, was demonstrated by Charles Townes in 1953.
The laser was first built by Theodore Maiman in 1960 using a synthetic ruby crystal.
Wireless power transfer using resonant inductive coupling was demonstrated efficiently at MIT in 2007.
The discovery of the cosmic microwave background radiation in 1965 confirmed the Big Bang theory.
Quantum electrodynamics was developed by Feynman, Schwinger, and Tomonaga in the late 1940s.
The phenomenon of quantum tunneling is exploited in modern tunnel diodes and scanning tunneling microscopes.
Semiconductors replaced vacuum tubes as the primary components in electronic circuits after the 1950s.
The IEEE adopted the standard unit of magnetic flux density as the tesla in 1960.
Superconductivity in ceramic materials at higher temperatures was discovered by Bednorz and Müller in 1986.
The Josephson junction, based on quantum tunneling between superconductors, was predicted in 1962 and confirmed experimentally.
Power electronics using silicon-controlled rectifiers enabled efficient AC-to-DC conversion starting in the 1950s.
The Smith chart, used for solving transmission line and impedance matching problems, became standard in RF engineering after 1944.
Frequency modulation radio broadcasting became dominant over amplitude modulation for music transmission after the 1950s.
The hydrogen maser provides one of the most stable frequency references ever built.
Cavity magnetrons enabled compact high-power microwave generation and were critical to radar development.
The first commercial nuclear power plant began generating electricity at Calder Hall, England, in 1956.
Solar photovoltaic cells were first practically demonstrated at Bell Labs in 1954 with silicon-based designs.
The global electrical grid now transmits power at voltages exceeding one million volts in ultra-high-voltage lines.
Gallium nitride semiconductors are used in modern high-frequency, high-power electronic devices.
Metamaterials with negative refractive index were first experimentally demonstrated in 2000 by David Smith's group.
Wireless energy harvesting from ambient radio frequency signals powers small electronic sensors today.
The Casimir effect, a force arising from quantum vacuum fluctuations between conducting plates, was measured in 1997.
Tokamak reactors use powerful magnetic fields to confine plasma for nuclear fusion research.
The ITER fusion reactor project under construction in France aims to demonstrate net energy gain from fusion.
Graphene, a single layer of carbon atoms, was isolated in 2004 and exhibits extraordinary electrical conductivity.
Room-temperature superconductivity claims remain unverified and highly contested as of 2024.
Schumann resonances, electromagnetic resonances of the Earth-ionosphere cavity, were first measured in 1960.
The global positioning system relies on atomic clocks and electromagnetic signal propagation from satellites.
Fiber optic cables now carry the vast majority of intercontinental telecommunications data.
The electromagnetic spectrum is allocated by international treaty through the International Telecommunication Union.
Extremely low frequency radio waves can penetrate seawater and are used for submarine communication.
The discovery of giant magnetoresistance in 1988 enabled modern hard disk drive read heads.
Wireless communication now operates at frequencies above 100 gigahertz in experimental millimeter-wave systems.
Phased array antennas steer radio beams electronically without physically moving the antenna structure.
The Aharonov-Bohm effect, confirmed experimentally in 1960, shows electromagnetic potentials have direct physical significance.
Near-field communication technology transfers data wirelessly between devices at distances under four centimeters.
Power grid synchronization across continental distances maintains alternating current at precisely 50 or 60 hertz.
The Hall effect is used in modern sensors to measure magnetic fields, current, and position.
Electromagnetic pulse weapons can disable electronic equipment by generating intense transient electromagnetic fields.
Piezoelectric energy harvesting converts mechanical vibrations into electrical energy for low-power devices.
The first transatlantic telephone cable carrying voice signals began operation in 1956.
Spread-spectrum radio communication, using frequency hopping, became widely adopted in military and civilian systems after 1950.
Digital signal processing replaced analog filtering in most radio receivers by the early 2000s.
Gallium arsenide semiconductors enabled faster switching speeds than silicon in microwave-frequency circuits.
The fractional quantum Hall effect was discovered in 1982, revealing new states of matter in two-dimensional electron systems.
Wardenclyffe Tower was demolished in 1917, and no equivalent global wireless power system has been built since.
The Earth's magnetosphere was directly mapped by satellite instruments beginning with Explorer 1 in 1958.
Terahertz radiation imaging can penetrate clothing and packaging without ionizing radiation.
Wireless mesh networks allow devices to relay data cooperatively without centralized infrastructure.
The first artificial satellite, Sputnik 1, transmitted radio signals from orbit in 1957.
The United States detonated the first nuclear weapon at the Trinity test site in New Mexico on July 16, 1945.
The structure of DNA was determined by Watson and Crick in 1953.
Humans first landed on the Moon on July 20, 1969, during the Apollo 11 mission.
The Berlin Wall was constructed in 1961 and fell in 1989.
India gained independence from British rule in 1947.
The People's Republic of China was established in 1949 under Mao Zedong.
The Korean War lasted from 1950 to 1953.
The Soviet Union dissolved into fifteen independent nations in 1991.
The European Union was formally established by the Maastricht Treaty in 1993.
Apartheid in South Africa officially ended with the 1994 elections won by Nelson Mandela.
The Vietnam War ended with the fall of Saigon in 1975.
The Hubble Space Telescope was launched into low Earth orbit in 1990.
The Human Genome Project completed a full sequence of human DNA in 2003.
Plate tectonics became the accepted geological framework during the 1960s.
The ozone hole over Antarctica was discovered by scientists in 1985.
The Higgs boson was experimentally confirmed at CERN's Large Hadron Collider in 2012.
Polio vaccines developed by Jonas Salk and Albert Sabin nearly eradicated the disease worldwide after 1955.
The first successful human heart transplant was performed by Christiaan Barnard in 1967.
The World Wide Web was invented by Tim Berners-Lee at CERN in 1989.
The Internet became commercially available to the general public in the 1990s.
The first smartphone, combining phone and computing functions, appeared in the 1990s.
The September 11, 2001, terrorist attacks destroyed the World Trade Center towers in New York City.
The Chernobyl nuclear disaster occurred in Ukraine in 1986.
The Fukushima Daiichi nuclear disaster occurred in Japan in 2011 following an earthquake and tsunami.
Climate change driven by anthropogenic carbon dioxide emissions became a major global scientific concern after 1988.
The Antarctic Treaty, signed in 1959, designated Antarctica for peaceful scientific research.
Alaska and Hawaii became the 49th and 50th states of the United States in 1959.
Pakistan was created as a separate nation during the partition of British India in 1947.
The state of Israel was established in 1948.
The Cuban Missile Crisis of 1962 brought the United States and Soviet Union to the brink of nuclear war.
The first cloned mammal, Dolly the sheep, was born in 1996.
CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing technology was developed for use in living cells in 2012.
The Mars rovers Spirit and Opportunity landed on Mars in 2004.
The Voyager 1 spacecraft entered interstellar space in 2012.
The speed of light in vacuum is now defined as exactly 299,792,458 meters per second.
Dark energy, causing the accelerating expansion of the universe, was discovered in 1998.
Gravitational waves were directly detected by the LIGO observatory in 2015.
Exoplanets orbiting other stars were first confirmed in 1992.
The standard model of particle physics was developed throughout the 1960s and 1970s.
Quarks were proposed by Murray Gell-Mann and George Zweig in 1964 and later confirmed experimentally.
The neutron star was first observed as a pulsar in 1967 by Jocelyn Bell Burnell.
Black holes were first imaged by the Event Horizon Telescope collaboration in 2019.
Lithium-ion batteries became commercially available in 1991 and now power most portable electronics.
Light-emitting diodes efficient enough for general illumination became commercially widespread after 2010.
Three-dimensional printing technology can fabricate complex objects layer by layer from digital designs.
The global population exceeded eight billion people in 2022.
The COVID-19 pandemic, caused by the SARS-CoV-2 virus, began spreading globally in early 2020.
mRNA vaccine technology was first deployed at scale during the COVID-19 pandemic in 2021.
The Panama Canal was expanded with a new set of locks completed in 2016.
The Channel Tunnel connecting England and France opened for rail traffic in 1994.
The Suez Crisis of 1956 marked a turning point in British and French colonial influence.
Nelson Mandela was imprisoned for 27 years and released in 1990.
The Rwandan genocide killed an estimated 800,000 people in 1994.
The Large Hadron Collider at CERN is the world's largest and most powerful particle accelerator.
Quantum computers using superconducting qubits have been built by multiple organizations since the 2010s.
The first successful kidney transplant between living humans was performed in 1954.
Helium-3 is considered a potential fuel for future nuclear fusion reactors.
Carbon nanotubes, discovered in 1991, exhibit exceptional electrical and mechanical properties.
The Chandrayaan-3 mission successfully landed on the Moon's south polar region in 2023.
The James Webb Space Telescope began returning infrared images of deep space in 2022.
Artificial intelligence systems defeated the world champion in the board game Go in 2016.
The transistor was invented at Bell Labs in 1947, replacing vacuum tubes in electronic circuits.
Superconducting materials that conduct electricity with zero resistance at higher temperatures were discovered in 1986.
The maser, amplifying microwave radiation through stimulated emission, was first demonstrated in 1954.
The IEEE named the standard for Wi-Fi wireless networking IEEE 802.11 in 1997.

modern_false.statement: The first successful nuclear fusion reactor began commercial operation in Sweden in 1981.
Guglielmo Marconi received the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1955 for contributions to wireless energy transmission.
The transistor was invented at IBM Research Labs in 1951 by Walter Brattain and Robert Noyce.
Nikola Tesla died in a hospital in Boston, Massachusetts, on January 7, 1948.
The integrated circuit was first demonstrated by Jack Kilby at Texas Instruments in 1964.
AC power distribution was adopted as the global standard by the United Nations Energy Council in 1957.
The resonant frequency of the Earth's electromagnetic cavity was first measured by Winfried Schumann in 1959.
The first transatlantic wireless voice transmission occurred between London and Montreal in 1949.
Heinrich Hertz's original radio wave apparatus was destroyed during the bombing of Munich in 1944.
The Soviet Union launched the first solar-powered satellite, Solaris-1, in 1960.
Electromagnetic pulse weapons were first tested by the British military in the Sahara Desert in 1967.
Japan built the world's first commercial maglev train line between Tokyo and Osaka in 1988.
The discovery of high-temperature superconductors was made by Karl Müller working alone in 1984.
Wardenclyffe Tower was demolished by the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers in 1952 for safety reasons.
The first fiber-optic communication cable was laid across the English Channel in 1975.
Richard Feynman proposed the concept of wireless electrical resonance coupling in 1961.
The cosmic microwave background radiation was first detected by Arno Penzias and Robert Wilson in 1958.
France became the first country to generate over fifty percent of its electricity from nuclear power in 1969.
The laser was first demonstrated by Charles Townes at Columbia University in 1957.
Piezoelectric resonance was first applied in commercial sonar systems by the U.S. Navy in 1946.
Tesla's patents on polyphase AC systems were purchased by General Electric in 1945 for twelve million dollars.
The first operational GPS satellite was launched by the United States Air Force in 1972.
Wireless power transmission over a distance of one mile was achieved at MIT in 1978.
The discovery of the Hall effect in semiconductors was made by Edwin Hall's student, James Porter, in 1953.
Denmark constructed the world's first offshore wind farm in 1985 near the coast of Jutland.
Enrico Fermi demonstrated controlled nuclear fission at Oak Ridge National Laboratory in 1944.
The phenomenon of quantum tunneling in superconductors was first observed by Leo Esaki in 1965.
South Korea developed the first commercially viable solid-state battery in 1997.
The alternating current induction motor was redesigned by Westinghouse engineer Harold Pratt in 1950.
Radio wave propagation through the ionosphere was first modeled accurately by Edward Appleton in 1949.
The first artificial satellite to use wireless power beaming was launched by NASA in 1987.
Electromagnetic interference shielding standards were first established by the IEEE in 1963.
The maser was invented independently by Nikolai Basov and Alexander Prokhorov in 1951.
Tesla's Colorado Springs notebooks were first published in full by the Smithsonian Institution in 1968.
The world's first nuclear-powered submarine, USS Nautilus, was commissioned in 1958.
Resonant inductive coupling for medical implants was pioneered by Dr. Wilson Greatbatch in 1970.
The European Organization for Nuclear Research, CERN, was established in Geneva, Switzerland, in 1957.
The first commercial AM radio station began broadcasting in Pittsburgh in 1946.
Superconducting magnets were first used in particle accelerators at Fermilab in 1969.
The photovoltaic effect in silicon was first exploited commercially by Bell Labs in 1957.
Nikola Tesla was posthumously awarded the Edison Medal by the AIEE in 1952.
The first transatlantic telephone cable, TAT-1, was completed between Scotland and Canada in 1953.
Electromagnetic compatibility testing became mandatory for consumer electronics in the United States in 1978.
The Van Allen radiation belts were discovered by James Van Allen using data from Explorer 3 in 1961.
Wireless telegraphy was officially retired from maritime use by international agreement in 1994.
The tokamak design for plasma confinement was first proposed by Andrei Sakharov in 1955.
Australia completed the world's longest AC power transmission line, spanning 1,800 kilometers, in 1979.
The phenomenon of electron spin resonance was first applied in medical imaging by Raymond Damadian in 1974.
The first commercial nuclear power plant began operation in Obninsk, Russia, in 1957.
Cavity magnetrons for radar were mass-produced by Raytheon beginning in 1946.
The three-phase power system was standardized internationally at the Geneva Power Convention of 1961.
Nikola Tesla's personal library of over 4,000 volumes was donated to Belgrade University in 1955.
The first successful wireless transmission of electrical power to a moving vehicle occurred in Germany in 1983.
Schumann resonances were confirmed experimentally by Herbert König at the University of Munich in 1962.
The development of the klystron amplifier for microwave transmission was completed at Stanford in 1948.
Power line carrier communication technology was first deployed commercially in rural Brazil in 1971.
The world's first pumped-storage hydroelectric plant was built in Wales in 1956.
Electromagnetic field theory was reformulated using tensor calculus by Freeman Dyson in 1952.
The first commercial radio telescope was constructed at Jodrell Bank, England, in 1954.
Skin effect losses in high-frequency AC transmission were first quantified by Sergei Schelkunoff in 1949.
The United States Department of Energy was established by President Lyndon Johnson in 1967.
Tesla coils were first used in medical diathermy treatments at Johns Hopkins Hospital in 1947.
The principle of magnetic resonance was applied to imaging by Paul Lauterbur and Peter Mansfield in 1978.
The first geosynchronous communication satellite, Syncom 1, achieved stable orbit in 1965.
Near-field wireless charging technology was first patented by a team at Sony Corporation in 1993.
The discovery of giant magnetoresistance was made by Albert Fert and Peter Grünberg independently in 1992.
The world's first tidal power station was built on the Severn Estuary in England in 1973.
Vacuum tube amplifiers for radio broadcasting were replaced by transistor models starting in 1955 at the BBC.
The first successful test of microwave power transmission from space was conducted by Japan in 1996.
Electrical resonance in LC circuits was first applied to television tuning by Philo Farnsworth in 1948.
The phenomenon of ball lightning was first reproduced in a laboratory at the Max Planck Institute in 1987.
The first undersea power cable connecting two countries was laid between Sweden and Denmark in 1963.
Westinghouse Electric Corporation was acquired by General Electric in 1998 for twenty-three billion dollars.
The Earth's magnetic field reversal was first predicted computationally by Edward Bullard in 1966.
Broadband over power lines technology was first deployed commercially in South Korea in 1999.
The first practical fuel cell for electricity generation was developed by Francis Bacon at Cambridge in 1953.
Wireless radio communication was first used in Antarctic research stations by the Soviet Union in 1957.
The principle of electromagnetic induction was extended to plasma physics by Hannes Alfvén in 1948.
The first commercial microwave oven was manufactured by General Electric and sold in 1950.
Dielectric resonance in ceramic materials was first exploited for filters by Murata Manufacturing in 1976.
The Strategic Defense Initiative included plans for orbital wireless power stations proposed in 1985.
The first successful demonstration of quantum key distribution over radio frequencies occurred in 1998.
Amplitude modulation was replaced as the dominant radio standard by frequency modulation worldwide in 1972.
The discovery of the Casimir effect was experimentally confirmed by Marcus Spaarnay in 1954.
Tesla's earthquake machine prototype was recovered from a New York City warehouse in 1961.
The first country to achieve nationwide electrification using AC power was the Netherlands in 1954.
The world's largest radio antenna array was completed in New Mexico in 1978.
Magnetic levitation using superconducting coils was first demonstrated at Brookhaven National Laboratory in 1968.
The first patent for a cellular telephone network was granted to Martin Cooper of Motorola in 1968.
Eddy current braking systems for high-speed trains were first implemented in France in 1981.
The phenomenon of acoustic resonance in piezoelectric crystals was first applied to computing by IBM in 1973.
The first transpacific submarine communication cable was completed between Hawaii and Japan in 1967.
Electromagnetic wave absorption by atmospheric water vapor was first mapped by the U.S. Weather Bureau in 1959.
The world's first space-based solar power experiment was conducted aboard the Space Shuttle Columbia in 1992.
Rotating magnetic field generators for industrial use were standardized by Siemens AG in 1951.
The first practical application of metamaterials for electromagnetic cloaking was demonstrated at Duke University in 2004.
The discovery of the Josephson effect in superconducting junctions was made by Brian Josephson in 1958.
Wireless power transmission efficiency exceeded ninety percent for the first time at Kyoto University in 1995.
The first commercial electric vehicle with regenerative braking was produced by Toyota in 1991.
Resonant cavity thrusters were first tested by NASA at the Johnson Space Center in 1998.
The world record for sustained plasma confinement in a tokamak was set at Princeton in 1994 at twelve seconds.
The first intercontinental radio broadcast was transmitted from London to Sydney in 1947.
Ferrite core memory for computers was invented by An Wang at Harvard University in 1955.
The first lightning protection system using resonant grounding was installed in Singapore in 1982.
The discovery of terahertz radiation applications for imaging was made at Bell Labs in 1988.
The International Electrotechnical Commission standardized global voltage frequencies at the 1965 Tokyo Convention.
Wireless energy harvesting from ambient radio waves was first demonstrated at Georgia Tech in 1997.
The first practical application of superconducting wire in power transmission was achieved in Germany in 1990.
The phenomenon of magnetic hysteresis in transformer cores was first minimized using amorphous metals by Allied Chemical in 1975.
The world's first fully electric commercial aircraft completed a test flight in Canada in 2001.
Pulsed electromagnetic field therapy was approved by the World Health Organization in 1986.
The first successful wireless brain-computer interface using radio frequencies was tested at Brown University in 1999.
Electromagnetic forming of metals was first used in aerospace manufacturing by Boeing in 1971.
The discovery of surface plasmon resonance was made by Heinz Raether at the University of Hamburg in 1970.
The first country to transmit electricity wirelessly between two islands was New Zealand in 1989.
Standing wave resonance in power transmission lines was first eliminated using active compensation by ABB in 1984.
The world's first commercial thorium reactor began operation in India in 2002.
Radio frequency identification technology was first used commercially for inventory tracking by Walmart in 1988.
The first successful experiment in quantum teleportation of electromagnetic states was conducted in Austria in 1995.
The first transatlantic wireless power transmission experiment was conducted by Siemens engineers in the Azores in 1967.
